Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only top‑level variable definitions and a `source` array with two legitimate download URLs: one from Mojang’s official launcher API and one from the maintainer’s GitHub repository (tagged release). No code is executed at source time beyond variable assignments. There is no `pkgver()` function, and no command substitutions, eval, or other dangerous operations that would run during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. All `build()` and `package()` logic is deferred and not triggered by the requested commands. The provided SHA‑512 checksums ensure integrity, and the source definitions follow standard AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code; safe to fetch and source.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code; safe to fetch and source.[/]

LLM auditresponse for minecraft-server.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard Arch Linux `.install` script used to display user-facing messages after package installation or upgrade. It defines variables for the game, user, and server root path, then contains `post_upgrade()` and `post_install()` functions that only `echo` informational strings. There are no network requests, no file modifications, no execution of external programs, no obfuscation, and no data exfiltration. The script purely provides guidance to the user (e.g., about switching from screen to tmux, where world data is stored, and how to accept the EULA). This is completely benign and in line with normal packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Benign informational install script; no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed minecraft-server.install. Status: SAFE -- Benign informational install script; no security issues.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard packaging practices for an AUR package. All external sources are downloaded from official or maintainer-controlled locations:
- The Minecraft server jar is obtained from `launcher.mojang.com` (Mojang's official CDN) with a pinned nonce and checksum.
- The helper script tarball is fetched from the maintainer's GitHub repository, also checksummed.

The build and package functions perform only expected operations: calling `make` on the upstream source, installing files into `$pkgdir`, creating symbolic links, and setting group-writable permissions on the server root. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests, no use of dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl|bash), and no modification of files outside the package's intended scope. The nonce substring in the source filename is cosmetic and harmless.

No evidence of data exfiltration, backdoors, or supply-chain injection was found. The use of a GitHub release tag (rather than a pinned commit) is ordinary for tarball sources and is mitigated by the provided checksum.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR packaging, no malicious behavior detected.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for minecraftd.tmpfiles.in:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a systemd-tmpfiles configuration template (`.tmpfiles.in`). It uses `z` and `d` directives to set ownership and permissions on the server root directory and its `logs` subdirectory. The placeholders `@SERVER_ROOT@` and `@GAME_USER@` are substituted during package installation. There is no executable code, no network access, no obfuscation, and no deviation from standard packaging practices. The file is purely declarative and serves the legitimate purpose of ensuring correct runtime directory permissions for the Minecraft server.
</details>
<summary>Standard tmpfiles config, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a systemd service template file for a Minecraft server. There are no executable commands, no network operations, no obfuscation, and no deviation from normal packaging practices. The placeholders (@GAME@, @INAME@, @GAME_USER@) are typical for a templated package like this. The service runs the server as a non-root user and group, which is appropriate.

LLM auditresponse for Makefile:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This Makefile is a standard build system for generating configuration files, systemd service units, and a shell script for a Minecraft server daemon. It uses `sed` to substitute predefined variables into `.in` template files and `install`/`rm` for deployment. There are no network requests, obfuscated code, or dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl, wget). All operations are confined to the package's own files and expected installation paths (`/usr`, `/etc/conf.d`, systemd directories). The code is transparent and follows routine packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Standard Makefile, no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for minecraftd-backup.service.in:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a systemd service unit template for triggering a world backup of a Minecraft server. It contains standard `[Unit]`, `[Service]`, and `[Install]` sections with placeholders for the game name, binary name, and user. There are no embedded commands, network requests, obfuscated code, or operations outside the scope of running a backup script (`/usr/bin/@INAME@ backup`). This file is consistent with legitimate packaging practices for Minecraft server AUR packages.
</details>
<summary>Standard systemd service template for Minecraft backup.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for minecraftd.sh.in:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a legitimate game server control script (Minecraft wrapper) that manages a tmux session, handles server start/stop/backup, and includes an idle daemon using netcat to listen for connections. All operations are within configured directories (`SERVER_ROOT`, `BACKUP_DEST`), all commands are standard bash with no obfuscation, no unexpected network requests, and no data exfiltration. The script sources a configuration file from `/etc/conf.d/`, which is normal packaging practice. There is no evidence of malicious code injection; any security concerns would stem from configuration management rather than the script itself.
</details>
<summary>Standard Minecraft server control script, no malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/10] Reviewing src/minecraft-server-1.0.4/minecraftd.sysusers.in...
+ Reviewed src/minecraft-server-1.0.4/minecraftd.sh.in. Status: SAFE -- Standard Minecraft server control script, no malicious code.
LLM auditresponse for minecraftd.sysusers.in:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a systemd sysusers.d template file. It defines a single system user with placeholder variables (@GAME_USER@, @GAME@, @SERVER_ROOT@) that will be substituted during the package build or install process. The syntax is standard for `sysusers.d` configuration: `u username id description home_directory shell`. There are no encoded strings, network operations, file manipulation, or any suspicious commands. The file is purely declarative and creates a dedicated user for the Minecraft server service, which is a common and expected packaging practice.
</details>
<summary>Standard sysusers template, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>
